source_metadata.title_raw: Robert N. Black - A-18-213 - October 25, 2018 - Eureka

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert N. Black 
City Attorney 
531 K Street 
Eureka, CA 95501-1165 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-213 
Dear Mr. Black: 
October 25, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Eureka City Councilmember 
Natalie Arroyo regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 
and Government Code Section I 090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act and Section I 090, not under other general 
conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract 
Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
· 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Humboldt County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Under Section 1090, if Councilmember Arroyo recuses herself, may the Eureka City 
Council approve a contract with the Redwood Community Action Agency ("RCAA")? 
2. Under the Act and Section 1090, if Councilmember Arroyo participates in the approval of 
a prime contract, may the prime contractor subsequently - without the prior knowledge of 
Councilmember Arroyo - select RCAA as a subcontractor or service provider? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Secti.ons 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-18-213 
Page No. 2 
1. Yes. Section 1090 prohibits Councilmember Arroyo from making or participating in the 
making of the potential agreements with RCAA; however, the City may enter into those agreements 
pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091(b)(l), as outlined below. 
Additionally, we note that Councilmember Arroyo must leave the room during the consideration of 
any such contracts pursuant to the Act's recusal requirements. 
2. No. Where Councilmember Arroyo participates in the approval of a prime contract, 
Section 1090 prohibits the City and general contractor from subcontracting with RCAA., The 
remote interest would not apply to decisions regarding the subcontractors because Councilmember 
Arroyo has participated in the contract and subcontracts in approving the initial contract. 
FACTS 
Councilmember Natalie Arroyo was,elected to the Eureka City Council in 2014 and is 
currently serving in that capacity. Councilmember Arroyo has worked at RCCA as a senior planner 
in the natural resources services division since 2007. She is an hourly employee and works slightly 
less than full time. RCAA is a source of income of more than $500 annually to Councilmember 
Arroyo. She does not receive bonuses except longevity bonuses awarded to all similarly situated 
employees and there is no profit sharing arrangement. Councilmember Arroyo does not have 
management or development responsibilities within the nonprofit organization. Following her 
election to the Eureka City Council, her work at RCAA is exclusively on projects outside of City of 
Eureka city limits. Currently, her work includes community health projects on the Samoa peninsula 
and in Bridgeville (southeastern Humboldt County). 
RCAA currently has a budget of about $8 million annually. The number of employees 
within its many divisions and AmeriCorps programs is in excess of one hundred and twenty-five 
individuals, although it has varied based on grant-funded programs and some short-term or seasonal 
employment (i.e., AmeriCorps members and field or maintenance workers). RCAA has been in 
existence since 1983 and is an Internal Revenue Code 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization. Community 
action agencies exist across the country and share a common goal of improving the lives of low-
income people. 
The City of Eureka is a charter city. Under its home rule powers, the City has adopted a 
subchapter of its Municipal Code that empowers the City Manager, acting as Purchasing Agent of 
the City, or the City Manager's designees to acquire public works, goods, and services without 
specific approval of the City Council below certain dollar thresholds. The basic precondition is that 
the City budget must contain the necessary appropriation for the acquisition in question. Above the 
thresholds, City Council approval is required. There are several categories of goods and services 
that fall under the Purchasing Agent's authority, each with its own dollar limits, and they include 
the following: 
§ 39.39 GOODS AND SERVICES. 
(D) Authority to approve contracts. All Contracts for goods and services valued at 
one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) or more require Council approval. 


File No. A-18-213 
Page No. 3 
(1) The City Manager is authorized, without Council approval, to approve 
and award a contract for goods and services valued at less than one hundred 
thousand dollars ($100,000). 
(2) The City Manager is authorized to delegate, in writing, authority to 
approve a contract for goods and services in an amount of ten thousand dollars 
($10,000) or less. 
§ 39.40 PROFESSIONAL SERVICES. 
(B) Negotiation procedure. For projects involving fees less than $100,000 where 
local business are well qualified to provide services, the Purchasing Agent, or 
designated Department may negotiate with a firm for services subject to prior 
approval from the Council. Otherwise, the Purchasing Agent shall utilize a 
request for qualifications or a request for proposals procedure for obtaining 
professional services. 
(C) Request for qualifications procedure. For projects involving fees of more than 
$100,000 or where it is in the city's best interest to request qualifications for 
services involving specialized area of expertise, a request for qualifications 
procedure shall be utilized. 
(D) Council approved required. All contracts for professional services more 
than$25,000 shall be approved by the Council. 
Because RCAA provides a wide-range of community services, elements of the organization 
are often called upon to provide service to the City of Eureka and to prime contractors and 
consultants to the City of Eureka. Examples include having RCAA facilitate public participation in 
planning endeavors and the production of interpretive signs for a City recreational trial. Also, the 
potential exists for prime contractors to the City to use RCAA services on a sub-contracted basis. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body ofa 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 


File No. A-18-213 
Page No. 4 
However, the Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's 
prohibition where the interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091 or 
a "noninterest," as defined in Section I 091.5. If a remote interest is present, the contract may be 
made by the agency prnvided: (1) the officer discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to 
the public body; (2) the interest is noted in the body's official records, and; (3) the officer abstains 
from participating in the making of the contract. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 158, 159 (2001).) 
Officer or Employee of a Nonprofit Corporation or 501 (c)(3) Entity. 
Section 1091(b)(l) sets forth a remote interest exception and provides in pertinent part as 
follows: 
"(b) As used in this article, "remote interest" means any of the following: ... 
"( 1) That of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from 
taxation pursuant to Section 501 ( c )(3) of the Internal Revenue ~ode (26 
U.S.C. Sec. 501(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c)(5) of the Internal Revenue 
Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 5°0l(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation." 
Councilmember Arroyo is an employee ofRCAA, an Internal Revenue Code 501(c)(3) 
nonprofit organization. Therefore, we find that she has a remote interest in potential agreements 
between the City and RCAA pursuant to Section 1091(b)(l). Because she has a remote interest in 
the potential agreements, the City may enter into those agreements that require approval of the City 
Council only if: (1) she discloses her financial interest in the agreements to the City; (2) her interest 
is noted in the City's official records; and (3) she abstains from any participation in the making of 
the agreements. (Section 109l(a); Brunner Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
106, 108 (2005).) 
Because we conclude that Councilmember Arroyo will have a "remote interest" in future 
contracts between the City and RCAA, we do not analyze this issue further under Section 1090. 
Additionally, because the remedy in this situation is for her to abstain from any participation in the 
approval of such contracts (see Section 109l(a)), we do not analyze the conflict of interest under the 
Act as the remedy for conflicts under the Act would not differ from the action already required, 
except to note that Councilmember Arroyo must leave the room during the consideration of any 
such contracts pursuant to the Act's recusal requirements. 
Further, you have asked whether Councilmember Arroyo may participate in approving 
contracts in those in instances where some or all of the subconsultants are not known or have not 
been retained by the principal consultant at the time the City Council approves the principal's 
contract and the possibility exists that RCAA will subsequently be retained as a subconsultant. 
Section 1090 equally applies to independent contractors. Courts have concluded that independent 
contractors, who serve in advisory positions that are often held by officers and employees, are 
subject to Section I 090. (See California Housing Financing Agency v. Hanover (2007) 148 
Cal.App.4th 682[ concluding that an independent contractor who performed a public function by 
participating in the making of contracts was an "employee" for purposes of inclusion under Section 


File No. A-18-213 
Page No. 5 
1090].) Therefore, in instances where Councilmember Arroyo has properly participated in 
approving a contract because it does not include RCAA as a subconsultant, Section 1090 will still 
apply to the City and general contractor as a prohibition against subsequently retaining RCAA as a 
subconsultant. The remote interest exception would not apply to decisions regarding the 
subcontractors because Councilmember Arroyo has participated in the contract and resulting 
subcontracts in approving the initial contract. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
J -0 ~
,=-,=,~' 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Councilmember Arroyo - select RCAA as a subcontractor or service provider?

QUESTION: 1. Under Section 1090, if Councilmember Arroyo recuses herself, may the Eureka City 
Council approve a contract with the Redwood Community Action Agency ("RCAA")? 
2. Under the Act and Section 1090, if Councilmember Arroyo participates in the approval of 
a prime contract, may the prime contractor subsequently - without the prior knowledge of 
Councilmember Arroyo - select RCAA as a subcontractor or service provider?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. Section 1090 prohibits Councilmember Arroyo from making or participating in the 
making of the potential agreements with RCAA; however, the City may enter into those agreements 
pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091(b)(l), as outlined below. 
Additionally, we note that Councilmember Arroyo must leave the room during the consideration of 
any such contracts pursuant to the Act's recusal requirements. 
2. No. Where Councilmember Arroyo participates in the approval of a prime contract, 
Section 1090 prohibits the City and general contractor from subcontracting with RCAA., The 
remote interest would not apply to decisions regarding the subcontractors because Councilmember 
Arroyo has participated in the contract and subcontracts in approving the initial contract.
